intro: The biggest real distinction between Datadog and Elastic is straightforward: Datadog carries high lock-in while Elastic has low lock-in. Datadog sits at usage-based pricing starting at $15/host/mo (Infra Pro), a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries high lock-in, while Elastic sits at pricing from $95/mo (Standard Cloud), a free tier, enterprise plan available and carries low lock-in. Datadog is a fit when you need enterprise — typical scenarios include enterprise needing unified apm + logs + metrics. Elastic is aimed at microservices performance monitoring, with workloads like microservices performance monitoring as the common case. The honest trade-off: Datadog is explicitly not for cost-sensitive; Elastic is not intended for simple, static projects. Lock-in is the factor most teams underestimate: Datadog and Elastic have different exit paths, and that shapes total cost of ownership over a two-to-three year horizon. Before committing, audit where each tool stores state, which APIs you depend on, and how painful a switch would be six months in.
quick_take: Datadog is for enterprise; Elastic is for microservices performance monitoring; decide based on how much lock-in you can accept.
verdict_a: Choose Datadog if your primary workload is enterprise and you prefer usage-based pricing (starts at $15/host/mo (Infra Pro)).
verdict_b: Choose Elastic if your primary workload is microservices performance monitoring and low switching cost matters.

faq.a: It depends on workload shape: Datadog lists $15/host/mo (Infra Pro), while Elastic lists $95/mo (Standard Cloud). With different pricing models (usage vs subscription), the cheaper option flips based on seat count, usage volume, and whether you use the free tier. Do a per-workload estimate rather than comparing headline numbers.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Datadog to Elastic?
faq.a: Migrating from Datadog to Elastic is not trivial — Datadog has high lock-in, which typically means data formats, APIs, or stack assumptions that have to be rewritten. Since both tools sit in the same category (Performance Monitoring / APM), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep Datadog running in parallel until Elastic is verified.

Our data gives Datadog and Elastic the same developer experience score (5/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Elastic a good alternative to Datadog?
faq.a: Elastic is in the same category as Datadog (Performance Monitoring / APM), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Elastic is aimed at microservices performance monitoring, while Datadog targets enterprise — that difference should drive the switch decision. Note that Elastic is explicitly not for simple, static projects — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
